retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The digastrics coordinate with other head and neck muscles during swallowing, chewing, and speech. The anterior belly of the digastric is 1 of 3 suprahyoid muscles stabilizing the hyoid during swallowing—an action that protects the airway while eating. The digastrics raise the tongue base and hyoid bone and depress the mandible for jaw opening, chewing, and speech. [5] Posterior digastric muscle contraction aids in head extension. The infrahyoids are the muscles antagonistic to the digastrics.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Digastric muscle trigger points may cause referred pain to the teeth. The therapy is of myofunctional type.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The digastrics are a pair of muscles so named as each has an anterior and a posterior belly (see Image . Digastric Muscle, Lateral View). These muscles derive embryonically from the 1st and 2nd pharyngeal arches and thus, are supplied by different nerves and arteries. The digastrics coordinate with other muscles in performing vital actions. Neuromuscular injuries can impair digastric muscle function, which may become apparent on physical examination.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The digastric muscle is implicated as the source of pain in bruxism or the habit of clenching teeth. Myofunctional physiotherapy or speech therapy can reduce the muscle's electrical activity and reduce pain during jaw movement.

retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the digastric muscle's anatomy, function, and potential involvement in various clinical conditions is crucial for healthcare professionals across different specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of the genioglossus muscle is to protrude the tongue anteriorly and deviate the tongue to the opposite side. [5] It also aids in swallowing as it will create a passage to the GIT. [5] When the left and right genioglossus muscles act together, they will depress the middle part of the tongue. [6] Specific parts of the genioglossus muscle are responsible for each of these important functions; for example, the anterior bundle of the genioglossus muscle is the portion of the muscle that is directly involved in protrusion of the tongue in the down and forward direction, whereas the inferior and posterior aspect of the muscle is responsible for moving the base of the tongue forward and elevating the core portion of the tongue as well. [3] [4] [6] The middle part of the muscle is specifically responsible for allowing the tongue to leave the oral cavity and protrude outwards. [3] [4] [6] When all of these specific areas of the genioglossus tongue act cohesively with one another, then the functions above can be completed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The digastric muscles may be implicated in post-radiation dysphagia or swallowing dysfunction. [10] Post-radiation changes leading to digastric atrophy or fibrosis is a possible sequela in patients who have undergone postoperative radiotherapy for oral cancer. [11] Exercises targeting the suprahyoid muscles, including the anterior belly of the digastric, are thought to help patients with dysphagia. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The neck contains a pair of digastric muscles, each subdividing into an anterior and posterior belly. The 2 bellies connect by an intermediate tendon. The anterior digastric belly attaches superiorly near the midline of the mandibular base on the digastric fossa and runs inferiorly toward the hyoid. The posterior belly attaches superiorly to the temporal bone at the mastoid notch and slopes inferiorly to meet the intermediate tendon (see Image . Neck Muscles).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: During the first action of swallowing when a bolus of food gets driven from the oropharynx into the pharynx, the hyoid bone and the tongue are carried upward and forward by the anterior belly of the digastric muscle, mylohyoid muscle, and the geniohyoid muscle. In the second act of swallowing, when the bolus is passing through the pharynx, the elevation of the hyoid bone is organized by the combined action of all these suprahyoid muscles. After the bolus has passed through the pharynx, the hyoid bone is driven superior and posterior by the posterior belly of the digastric muscle and the stylohyoid muscle to prevent the regurgitation of food back into the oral cavity. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The mylohyoid muscle maintains a critical participant in day to day activities, including mastication and swallowing of food and production of speech in conversations. A key mechanism of action of the mylohyoid muscle is it works directly and indirectly with the infrahyoid muscle to guide the position of the hyoid bone. When the mylohyoid muscle contracts during swallowing, it functions to elevate the base of the tongue and the hyoid bone anterosuperior with the mandible fixed. Per these actions, the mylohyoid muscle also depresses the mandible and elevates the oral cavity if the hyoid bone is in a fixed position from the action of its depressors. The mylohyoid muscle acts as a support structure to reinforce the base of the oral cavity by forming the oral diaphragm. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Suprahyoid muscles: the suprahyoid muscles are composed of the digastrics, stylohyoid, mylohyoid, and geniohyoid. During swallowing, these muscles act to elevate the hyoid bone and the base of the tongue. Additionally, they function to depress the mandible when the hyoid bone is in a fixed position. The mylohyoid branch of the inferior alveolar nerve supplies the anterior belly of the digastric muscle and the mylohyoid muscle. The facial nerve provides innervation to the posterior belly of the digastric muscle and the stylohyoid muscle. Nerve fibers from C1 innervate the geniohyoid muscle. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The mylohyoid muscle originates from the mylohyoid line of the mandible and inserts on the body of the hyoid bone. The mylohyoid muscle functions to depress the mandible and elevate both the oral cavity and the hyoid bone. The stylohyoid muscle has its origin from the styloid process of the temporal bone and inserts on the lateral surface of the body of the hyoid bone at its junction with the greater cornu. The contraction of the stylohyoid muscle elevates and retracts the hyoid bone, especially during swallowing. Two bellies comprise the digastric muscle; there are the anterior belly and the posterior belly of the digastric muscle. The posterior belly of the digastric muscle originates from the mastoid notch, which is a deep groove between the mastoid process and the styloid process of the temporal bone and inserts on the body of the hyoid bone. The anterior belly of the digastric muscle originates from the digastric fossa on the posterior surface of the mandible and inserts on the body of the hyoid bone. The contracture of the digastric muscles causes elevation of the hyoid bone, or depression of the mandible if the hyoid bone is being locked in place by the strap muscles. The geniohyoid muscle originates from the mental spine of the mandible and inserts on the body of the hyoid bone. Contraction of the geniohyoid muscle cause elevation and forward protrusion of the hyoid bone along with depression of the mandible. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Several cervical muscles act extrinsically on the larynx to aid in phonation and swallowing. Muscles inserted on the superior aspect of the hyoid (geniohyoid, digastric, mylohyoid, thyrohyoid, and stylohyoid muscles) and pharynx (stylopharyngeus, palatopharyngeus, and pharyngeal constrictor muscles) act in conjunction to elevate the larynx. Those muscles inserted on the inferior surface of the hyoid (sternohyoid and omohyoid muscles) and the sternothyroid muscle act to depress the larynx. Physical examination maneuvers to examine the thyroid often involve the patient voluntarily swallowing to localize this gland better because of laryngeal elevation during swallowing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The primary and accessory muscles of mastication work in a coordinated fashion to produce mandibular movement for chewing food.  The accessory muscles of mastication are the buccinator, suprahyoid muscles (digastric muscle, mylohyoid muscle, and geniohyoid muscle), and infrahyoid muscles (the sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid and omohyoid muscle). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: The gastrostomy site is situated in the stomach (žaludek). The surgical procedure labeled “Incision and excision of stomach” includes a subordinate procedure called فغر المعدة and is directly related to the stomach (žaludek). This procedure is classified under the broader category of gastrectomy, encompassing both partial and total resections. In addition to serving as the anatomical location for the gastrostomy site, the stomach (žaludek) is also identified as a site for injections (حقن) and plays a role in gastrointestinal regulation (تنظيم الجهاز الهضمي). Together, these relationships highlight the central importance of the stomach in various surgical interventions, therapeutic administrations, and physiological regulatory processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrostomy site is anatomically located in the stomach (žaludek). The procedure termed Incision and excision of stomach is directly related to the stomach and is categorized under gastrectomy, encompassing both partial and total forms of this surgery. Additionally, the stomach can serve as a location for injections (حقن), indicating that certain therapeutic or diagnostic injections may be administered within the gastric region. Together, these connections illustrate how the stomach functions as a central focus for various surgical interventions, such as gastrostomy and gastrectomy, as well as for specific injection-based treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, identified in English as “biopsy of liver,” in Arabic as “خزعة الكبد,” and in Czech as “játra,” is linked to a specific diagnostic intervention. The term “biopsy of liver” classifies the Arabic phrase “خزعة الكبد,” indicating that both refer to the same liver biopsy procedure. The anatomical entity “játra” is described as having a partially excised anatomy procedure called “خزعة الكبد,” meaning that a liver biopsy involves the partial removal of liver tissue. Moreover, “játra” is noted as an indirect procedure site for other non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, highlighting that the liver can serve as a target for a variety of therapeutic interventions beyond those directly involving the gastrointestinal tract. This integrated view clarifies multilingual terminology and underscores the broader procedural relevance of the liver in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2251
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, referred to as játra, can function as an indirect site for a range of other non‑or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, indicating that it may be affected or involved in treatments that are not primarily directed at the gastrointestinal system. In contrast, the liver is also a direct site for the diagnostic procedure فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية (ultrasound examination of the liver), which specifically targets hepatic tissue for imaging and assessment. This dual role underscores the liver’s relevance both as a secondary participant in broader therapeutic interventions and as a primary focus of dedicated hepatic imaging.

Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) represents liver inflammation and is clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), a cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts that is further related to jaundiced liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). Liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد) is a partially excised‑anatomy procedure that obtains tissue for histologic evaluation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 406
retrieved_docs.content: The abdomen destructive procedure is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the abdomen (břicho) and is classified as a subtype of excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions. This procedure therefore has a parent relationship with excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions, indicating that it shares the same therapeutic goals of removing tissue and separating adhesions within the peritoneal cavity. While the procedure directly targets the abdominal region, it also involves the peritoneum indirectly, reflecting the anatomical continuity between the abdominal wall (břicho) and the peritoneal lining. The term břicho (the abdomen) encompasses several related concepts. It includes the sub‑entity بَطْنٌ حادّ, representing a specific abdominal condition, and is associated with a palpable mass in the abdomen (كتلة في البَطْن). Diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum is a key imaging relationship for evaluating břicho, providing clinicians with detailed visualization of abdominal structures and potential pathologies. The peritoneum, as the serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity, is an indirect procedural site for excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions. Pathological findings on the peritoneum include abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), indicating that herniation can be identified at the peritoneal level. Additionally, the peritoneum is linked to intestinal disturbance (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ), underscoring its role in gastrointestinal functional abnormalities. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how abdominal surgical procedures, imaging modalities, and peritoneal pathology are connected in the assessment and management of abdominal disorders, emphasizing the importance of a comprehensive approach that considers both direct and indirect anatomical sites.

This relationship indicates that a pericardial biopsy is a particular type of intervention that falls under the larger umbrella of operations targeting both the heart muscle and its surrounding sac, highlighting its role as a focused diagnostic or therapeutic maneuver within comprehensive cardiac surgical care.
